The Profit Center 	1_Kick_Your_Business_Into_Hyper-drive 	Page 1 of 30
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 1 of 30
Bob Bly: 	My name is Bob Bly, and I’m interviewing Clayton Makepeace
who is one of the handful of copywriters in the world – freelance
copywriters, who makes more than a $1,000,000.00 a year, and
some years $2,000,000.00 or $3,000,000.00, strictly as a freelance
copywriter.
He’s a copywriter and he is the, I believe, the highest paid
freelance copywriter in the world, and one of only, as I would say,
a handful, maybe half a dozen, that are in seven figures. There’s a
bunch of us, below Clayton, who are earning six figures, anywhere
from $100,000.00 to $700,000.00 – $800,000.00 a year.
But he’s in the million dollar category. So this is, to my
knowledge, the first time ever, we have an opportunity to learn
from a million dollar copywriter; his secrets for being outrageously
successful as a freelance copywriter. So without further delay, I
just wanna dive into it ‘cause we have, today, an hour.
Now, the one thing I wanted to get to, in terms of housekeeping,
before we started, not everyone came to this through “The Total
Package” or through your E-letter; some came from other sources.
So you do publish a – now, Clayton, an E-newsletter, correct?
Clayton Makepeace: 	That’s correct.
Bob Bly: 	What’s the name of it?
Clayton Makepeace: 	We just started it in June, and it’s called “Makepeace Total
Package” – actually, it’s called “The Total Package.” And the – if
anyone on the call hasn’t subscribed, the subscription is free and
you’re welcome to point your browser to “Makepeace Total
Package” dot com.
That’s “M-A-K-E-P-E-A-C-E Total Package,” no spaces, dot com,
and you’ll find an opt-in form there that will get you our weekly
issues, from now on, free.
Bob Bly: 	So ‘ya gotta get that weekly, but let’s get right into it. Lots of
people there – when I started copywriting 25 years ago, I – it was
like an unheard-of profession. There were few people doing it, but
no one knew what it is. But, now, it’s sort of a hot thing, and
WARNING:
The following transcript is raw, uncut, and may contain grammatical errors.

-- 1 of 30 --

The Profit Center 	1_Kick_Your_Business_Into_Hyper-drive 	Page 2 of 30
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 2 of 30
everybody’s sayin’, “Oh, this is the greatest job going.” Is that
true? Is it really good?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Well, I think it is. I started back in the early ‘70s after having
worked in television and film for a while, and found, in ‘74, that
there was no work then because of the recession. And so I found a
job with an ad agency for $15,000.00 a year; that doesn’t sound
like much but I had no training, and I had no experience, and I was
a high school dropout.
And, after all, $15.000.00 a year in ‘74 is the same as $62,000.00
in 2005 dollars. So it wasn’t a bad [Laughter] way to start, and
within six years, I was doing over a quarter – well, doing very
close to a $250,000.00 a year in today’s money.
Within eight years, I was over $700,000.00 in today’s money; and
in the early ‘90s, I hit $1,000,000.00 a year and never looked back.
Bob Bly: 	So did ‘ya have a mentor or a coach who gotcha into that? How
did you discover how to do this?
Clayton Makepeace: 	No, that’s the beauty of it. I had a few books that I read: Reality in
Advertising by Rosser Reeves; Scientific Advertising by Claude
Hopkins; Tested Advertising Methods by John Caples; Ogilvie on
Advertising and Confessions of an Advertising Man by David
Ogilvie.
And, then, to get the nuts and bolts of the business, I read
Successful Direct Marketing Methods by Bob Stone. And that was
my foundation in the business, and, then, after that, I just really
started watching what people were mailing and studied each
package very carefully.
Today, everybody on this call, I think, is far more fortunate than
[Laughter] I was because I had to learn and make it up as I went
along. There weren’t coaches, there were E-zines telling you how
to do it, there was nothing, and so, I think, copywriters starting
today, really have a leg up.
Also, a lotta top copywriters today are taking on copycubs and
doing mentoring with them. As a way to extend their own income,
they’re accepting beginning writers as copycuts and training them.
I’ve done that six or seven times now, and each one of those
writers is a mid-to-high six-figure writer or million dollar writer.
Bob Bly: 	Can ‘ya name a couple of them or is it confidential?

-- 2 of 30 --

The Profit Center 	1_Kick_Your_Business_Into_Hyper-drive 	Page 3 of 30
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 3 of 30
Clayton Makepeace: 	Not at all – no, Carline Anglade Cole, I think my readers are
familiar with it – the name, came to me in the mid-1990s. She was
making six figures a year as a marketing assistant with XX
Publishing. But she was not a trained copywriter by any sorts of
the imagination, and she asked me if, as a friend, I would help her
break into this business and I did.
And her very first year, she made more as a copywriter than she
had been making with her six-figure salary at Phillips. Kent
Komae came to me – he was a five-figure writer, just getting
started, and I hired him to help me with some work I had at Weiss
Research.
And Kent is now a solid six-figure writer; the same is true with
Bob Hutchinson. Parris Lampropoulos took off like a house of fire
and is making seven figures now as a copywriter and has his own
copycuts, and there are a couple of others.
Brian Lundin who, now, [Laughter] is a refugee from New
Orleans living in Baton Rouge after that hurricane, made enough
money as a copywriter that he bought Jefferson Financial from his
boss and now runs a New Orleans Investment Conference and has
several newsletters and trading services that he publishes.
I hired Brian from a radio station where he was writing spots for
local customers – clients. So of the six or seven people I’ve
worked with over the last five or six years, every one of them has
done very, very well for themself.
And so – you know, I’ve just – I’ve seen it – it’s happened to me,
I’ve seen it happen to other people. It’s great.
Bob Bly: 	And – so you’re not a – an anomaly, as they say, in the matrix. It
can be done.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Not only can it be done, but [Laughter] these young guys who –
are doing it fast than I [Laughter] did. You know, it took me a
considerable amount of time to hit the $1,000,000.00 mark, and
Parris did it in a matter of a – just a few years.
Bob Bly: 	Now, for you speaking of people who start out, who are younger or
starting out maybe after another career, the guys you all mention,
work for major clients. But for many people listening to this call,
especially novices, the idea of going out and contacting and selling
itself to a major client, is very intimidating.

-- 3 of 30 --

The Profit Center 	1_Kick_Your_Business_Into_Hyper-drive 	Page 4 of 30
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 4 of 30
But how – for example, when you started, how did you overcome
this?
Clayton Makepeace: 	It was a tough one for me because the – right now, promoting “The
Total Package” means I have to promote myself and my
background – my experience. That’s that hardest thing I’ve done
in, probably, 20 years ‘cause it’s just not my nature to do that.
The same was true when I was getting started with copywriting.
The – I had tremendous insecurities and stage fright, and when I
would be faced with – fortunately, I was unemployed when I went
[Laughter] freelance. By definition, I was unemployed, and I had
a wife and two kids and an overhead I had to meet every month.
And so, I would wakeup on the first of the – each month, with no
money coming in, and I had no choice but to bang the phones and
to contact clients and say, “Here I am.” But I had a lot of problem
with that.
It was not easy because I’d be talking to a marketing manager at
KCI, which was Bob Kephart’s company back in the old days, and
I would have seen their promotion pieces for months and was
impressed by them and was studying them and trying to learn from
[Laughter] them.
And here I was on the phone with the copy chief who directed the
production of that piece; it was pretty daunting. But I found out,
very early on, that they were more interested or, at least, as
interested – most cases, more interested in talking to me than I was
to them because their jobs were dependent on finding great
copywriters to promote their product.
Bob Bly: 	But at the time, you may have been great in terms of your raw
talent, but you were not a proven commodity at – I mean, a proven
force at that time, were you?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Absolutely not.
Bob Bly: 	So why would they talk to you?
Clayton Makepeace: 	It all turned around for me when I realized that sales copy is the
one missing ingredient that these companies need, to promote their
existing products and to add new products.

-- 4 of 30 --

The Profit Center 	1_Kick_Your_Business_Into_Hyper-drive 	Page 5 of 30
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 5 of 30
They can’t grow without great sales copy and that – after I learned
that, I learned that these people have committees and meetings and
managers whose only job is to go out there, beat the bushes and
find the next hot copywriter.
And if you can come in with some good ideas, a couple of good
samples of your work, and then talk to them about how you would
improve what they’re doing now; give them a few ideas. It costs
them more not to test you than it does to go ahead and give you a
try.
Bob Bly: 	So they’re – so you’re saying, which I think is the revelation to a
lotta people on this call, that rather than – when the copywriters’
on this call contact these publishers, rather than –
[Audio interruption at 10:28]
Bob Bly: 	__________ annoyant, we’re actually helping them do their job.
Their look – they – their job is to look for this.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah, when a marketing manager at a major direct marketing
companies – direct response company, a publisher, a vitamin sales
company, whatever, finds a copywriter who proves to be an asset
to that company, it’s a feather in that marketing manager’s hat.
The person who first hired me to do a health promotion at Phillips
– the person who first hired Gary Bencivenga to do a financial
promotion at Phillips, these people now are heroes. They’ve got –
finding great writers got them the corner offices, the company cars,
the big bonuses and all the things that go along with moving up in
a company.
Bob Bly: 	Well, I understand they’re looking, but when you’re a beginner and
you call them, beyond the fact that they’re looking, then the
question becomes, “Well, why should they be interested in you?”
How did you overcome that as a beginner? Did you have some
great samples? Did you create some great samples?
How – once – obviously, they had a need, but how did you sell
you, Clayton, as the solution to that need? I understand how you
do it now – you have a great track record. But back then, I don’t
think ‘ya did. You were a –
Clayton Makepeace: 	No –
Bob Bly: 	Beginner, right?

-- 5 of 30 --

The Profit Center 	1_Kick_Your_Business_Into_Hyper-drive 	Page 6 of 30
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 6 of 30
Clayton Makepeace: 	No, I didn’t. The first thing that I did, really – in fact, everyone
who’s on this call, I hope, has received the two bonus reports that
we offered with the call; and one of the bonuses is “How to Get
Paid to Build a Power-Packed Portfolio.” And in there, I talk
about exactly how I did it Bob; there were several ways.
One was the challenge. I picked up a phone one day – I remember
I had $70.00 in the bank because I had just returned a rental truck
that I’d [Laughter] used to move my family from Los Angeles to
Prescott, Arizona. I picked up the phone and I called the publisher
I knew of in Phoenix, who I was just dying to write for.
He had seven or eight newsletters that he promoted. It was Johnny
Johnson at Research Publications, and I just told him on the phone
that I thought his package stunk and that I could beat it. By the
time I got off the call, I had an assignment.
Now, I had offered him to write the package for him for free and
that he would only pay me if it worked, and I felt, at the time, I had
to make an offer like that for that very reason. He didn’t know me;
I didn’t have a big portfolio.
I had some direct marketing promotions I had written, including an
audition I had done earlier for an advertising agency and a few
product promos for everything – you know, water heaters, a
restaurant, Beverly Hills, those kinds of things, that I had picked
up.
But I had no newsletter promotion, and so, I basically used this –
an offer that was too good to refuse, “Let me spend a month of my
life tryin’ to create a breakthrough for you, and if it doesn’t work,
you don’t owe me a cent.”
Bob Bly: 	So it was kinda risk-free?
Clayton Makepeace: 	It was risk-free for him; it was a no brainer.
Bob Bly: 	And he went for it, it sounds like –
Clayton Makepeace: 	He absolutely went for it, and within a year, I was doing
promotions for all seven of his newsletters.
Bob Bly: 	Now, the question would be though, that was then, this is now. A
lotta copywriters email me and they say, “I’m very worried. I look
on the Internet and it seems there is all these all AWAI students

-- 6 of 30 --

The Profit Center 	1_Kick_Your_Business_Into_Hyper-drive 	Page 7 of 30
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 7 of 30
and, you know, there’s all these copywriting boot camps and
there’s so many copywriters today.”
So, now, is it different? Is there too many copywriters? Or is the
demand still greater than the supply in copywriting? You would
know, so tell us.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Sure, I had a conversation with Ed Elliott at Phillips Publishing
today. He’s our Creative Director; he’s in charge of finding new
talent, and – in fact, he’s been calling me, pursuing me, at least
weekly, for the last month, to help him find new writers and
they’re desperate. Now, this is Phillips, okay?
They pay very substantial or – not, I’m sorry, not Phillips anymore,
it’s Healthy Directions now; I have to remember that. But they
pay substantial royalties; they pay solid advances; they’re willing
to try young writers; and they’re desperate for new talent.
They have a very large product line; they’re wanting to add new
product; they can’t do it until they get new writers. They’re
desperate and Phillips is one of the Roles Royce’s in the whole
publishing industry.
So I know that it’s still the case. Now, if you go in there and you
have nothing that has mailed for another client, but you do for –
especially for another newsletter or supplement company. But you
have an audition piece you wrote for –
Bob Bly: 	Explain what you mean by an “audition piece?”
Clayton Makepeace: 	An audition piece would be something that I think our AWAI
students’ are very, very familiar with.
Bob Bly: 	We call them “spec assignments.”
Clayton Makepeace: 	Right. A spec – well – there’s spec assignments and there’s spec
assignments. They’re the – the project that you actually do as a
student of AWAI, and there’s some other writing courses as well.
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	And as part of your education, you have created these assignments.
Then there are the spec assignments that – where you actually look
at a product in a promotion piece that, let’s say, Phillips is doing.
You pick a newsletter, let’s say it’s “Health and Healing,”
___________ Whitaker’s newsletter.

-- 7 of 30 --

The Profit Center 	1_Kick_Your_Business_Into_Hyper-drive 	Page 8 of 30
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 8 of 30
And you look at the package they’re mailing to promote that, and
you take as long as you want because they don’t know you’re
doing it –
Bob Bly: 	[Laughter]
Clayton Makepeace: 	And you write the best promotion that newsletter has ever had; the
best promotion you know how to write. And, then, once it’s done,
you send it to Ed Elliott, over at Phillips, or you do it for Agora.
You send it to Jenny Thompson at Health Group, at Agora or one
of the other creative directors or group publishers at these major
publishing companies.
You do that, you tell them that you’d like to talk to them about it,
and you get them on the phone and you say, “Look,” you know, “I
just wrote this package for you. I think its got great potential; I
think it can beat what you’re mailing now.” And one of several
things will happen.
They’ll either say, “Absolutely, we want this package. How much
you want for it?” That’s when you get to tell them what your deal
is, and we can get to that in a minute. Or they’ll say, “You know,
it’s close but no cigar. But, you know, we’d like to work with you
a little bit on it; we think it has potential.”
And, now, you’ve got a copy chief at a major publisher who’s
gonna work with you through the copy. Can be a – they’re,
basically, gonna pay you to learn. Now, there are two educations;
there’s the education that you pay for, and there’s the education
you get in the real world where people pay you [Laughter].
And that’s what starts when this kind of thing happens. The worst
thing they can do is just, basically, say, “You know what? We’ve”
– and they’ll be nice, believe me. [Laughter] They’ll say, “Well,
we just don’t have need for a writer right now.”
They’re lyin’ through their teeth, but they’re tryin’ to be nice to
‘ya. What they’re really saying is, “You’re not quite there yet.”
Bob Bly: 	So what do ‘ya do in that case?
Clayton Makepeace: 	You say, “That’s great. Well, you know” – and ask them for
suggestions. “What do you think I could do to become the kinda
writer that you would wanna hire?” And these people are nice

-- 8 of 30 --

The Profit Center 	1_Kick_Your_Business_Into_Hyper-drive 	Page 9 of 30
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 9 of 30
people; you know, after 30 years with these folks – they’re friends
of mine, they went to my wedding.
They, you know – when we get on the phone, we chat for way
much longer than we should [Laughter] about family and about
other things. These are nice people, and they’re highly motivated
to discover the next great writer. So my suggestion is, take a deep
breath and take the bullet and say, “You know what?”
“I really appreciate that as” – you know, “You’re being quite
honest with me here. I was just wondering what I – what you
might I might do; who I might talk to; what were the weak spots
you saw on the copy?” You know, pick their brain, and they’ll be
more than happy to talk to you about it.
Bob Bly: 	Now, if they don’t jump on what you’ve sent ‘em and say, “Yeah,
we wanna mail this; how much?” Is there – how can you convince
the reluctant client who’s maybe sitting on the fence and says,
“Well, maybe you could work out,” to give you that shot?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Well, that’s kinda the art of the deal again. You can simply offer
to finish the package to their satisfaction for no upfront fee and for
a royalty. You can offer to do this one for them at a substantially
reduced royalty whereas, normally, you would ask $30.00 a 1,000
for a health package for $40.00 a 1,000.
I think $30.00 is about reasonable. The – on this one you’ll say,
“Look, I tell ‘ya what. Not only ___________. You help me
finish this; you copy chief me to finish this, and I’ll only take
$10.000 a 1,000. I know it’ll take a little longer, it’ll take a little
more of your time, but I think it’s a good investment.”
And I think you’d be surprised how many people will accept that
kind of an offer.
Bob Bly: 	So let’s look at those fee arrangements. First, there is the royalty
only, and you’re saying a royalty can range from anywhere from
$10.00 to $30.00 per 1,000.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Sure. Hey, Bob, before I jump into –
Bob Bly: 	Yeah –
Clayton Makepeace: 	That, let me just add one last thing –
Bob Bly: 	Sure –

-- 9 of 30 --

The Profit Center 	1_Kick_Your_Business_Into_Hyper-drive 	Page 10 of 30
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 10 of 30
Clayton Makepeace: 	Here. You know, when you do one of those spec assignments, say
you do with the one for “Health and Healing” we were just
discussing.
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	When you send it in to Ed Elliott over at Phillips, go ahead and
send it to Jenny Thompson and other people too. And just tell
them, “Here’s a package I’m creating for” – you know, “For
Phillips at ‘Health and Healing.’” You know, “Here’s a – it’s a
rough draft still, but it’s in process and it’s a sample of my work.”
So, you know, the thing that you wrote for Phillips could generate
money for you, but it – from Phillips, but it could also be a sample
or a sales tool that you can show around the industry, and let
people know that you’re a serious writer and that you’re talking to
people who are hiring serious writers.
Bob Bly: 	If you show it to people – let’s say you do it for a Phillips
publication and you show it to people, other than Phillips, like,
Agora or Soundview. Do you tell them it’s a spec assignment? Or
do you not say anything about it?
Clayton Makepeace: 	I would –
Bob Bly: 	Would –
Clayton Makepeace: 	Not. You know if they ask about what kind of arrangements that
you have with Phillips, I would absolutely go ahead and be honest.
It’s a very small and incestuous business. They – all of the
competitors know each other. They go to the same conferences,
and they talk to each other, and they compare notes on
copywriters.
They’ll never tell you – if they have a hot new copywriter, they
will not tell the competitor that. But what they will them is if
they’re working with a copywriter and it’s well-known, and the –
you know, they’ll say, “Well, you know, he’s spotty,” or, you
know, “Not consistent,” or “He’s really good” – if that’s already
[Laughter] well-known.
So – but just be aware that these – your clients talk to each other;
they hang out with each other; they go to the same conferences.
And anything you say to a client, there’s a very, very good chance
it’s going to be repeated to the other perspective client.

-- 10 of 30 --

The Profit Center 	1_Kick_Your_Business_Into_Hyper-drive 	Page 11 of 30
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 11 of 30
The other thing is you have to be a little careful when you’re doing
this kinda thing because you don’t wanna, you know, post it on the
Internet or, you know, blast it out in big mailings to people because
if Phillips does accept your work and give you an assignment,
then, you know, they’re not going to appreciate the fact that you’ve
taken copies or about to mail and showed it to all of their
competitors.
But so long as they don’t have a commitment to you, so long as
they haven’t told you that they are absolutely gonna nail it, you can
get by with it. Just be a little bit subtle about [Laughter] it.
Bob Bly: 	That makes sense. Now –
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yep –
Bob Bly: 	You are, I think, ‘cause I’ve heard you, a really good negotiator
and you seem to negotiate and arrange all your own deals. And
you’ve said to me that there about, you know, half-a-dozen
different combinations and permutations of fee arrangements for
copywriters, under which the top people work. Can you run
through those for us?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Sure. When I first got started, basically, we were working on flat
fees, believe it or not, in the ‘70s [Laughter]. To write a direct
mail package, I got $600.00 flat, and that was to write an 8 to 16
page sales letter.
Everything was envelopes back then; nobody used – in our
industry, at least, was using self-mailers, magalogs and tabloids.
And so, I got $600.00 to do it, and to add insult to injury, there was
no Federal Express. So if I had to get something to a client
quickly, I had to buy a seat on an airplane for it.
The only fax machines we had were these little Quip machines,
and the – it took three minutes to transmit one page and you had to
load each page separately, and they stunk to high heaven
[Laughter] when they were doing it. And we were tight – we were
writing on IBM Selectric typewriters.
So every draft and then a complete retyping of the entire project,
and for that, we got $600.00 flat fee. For new writers, it’s –
especially working with the Internet, and especially working with
local clients and companies that are not familiar with royalty

-- 11 of 30 --

The Profit Center 	1_Kick_Your_Business_Into_Hyper-drive 	Page 12 of 30
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 12 of 30
arrangements, a flat fee is a good option because it’s a great way to
get some samples in your portfolio.
Bob Bly: 	So flat fee.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yes. Another way to go is royalty only, which we discussed. It’s
a great way for a young copywriter to get going. You simply go in
and you say, like, “I’m not even gonna charge you in advance
‘cause you have no way of knowing how great my copy’s gonna
be, and the fact that it’s gonna earnout like crazy.”
And earnout is, you know, when a publisher pays you in advance,
on your royalties. He calculates how many pieces he’s gonna have
to mail of that, in order to reimburse him for the advance he paid
you.
So a lotta packages don’t earnout; he charge ‘ya $25,000.00
advance and a $25.00 a 1,000 royalty, they’re gonna have to mail –
what is it, a 1,000,000 pieces –
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	In order to get their money back, right? So a lotta packages don’t
earnout. So – and that’s particularly a problem with young and
proven writers, so doing – you know, offering to write for a royalty
only is an excellent way to build your portfolio and to get going.
Bob Bly: 	Okay, so flat fee then royalty only, and then you mentioned the
idea of an advance plus royalty. Is that what most people do?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Most of the people I know, work on an advance plus royalty, and
essentially, the advance and the royalty are established in
negotiation between the copywriter and client. And from a
copywriter’s standpoint, you’re interested in how large this guy’s
mailing universe is, and, you know, what the mailing potential is.
If you hit an outta the park homerun for the guy. If you do as well
as his control or of – or slightly better, then, you know, how many
pieces is he capable of mailing given his mailing universe – the
mailing universe, of course, is the total number of qualified
prospects to the product that you’re promoting that – if you’re on
mailing lists or email lists.
And so if the mailing universe is gigantic, I generally give my
clients a bit of a break on the royalty amount and so forth. For
example, the health market, where there’s 280,000,000 Americans

-- 12 of 30 --

The Profit Center 	1_Kick_Your_Business_Into_Hyper-drive 	Page 13 of 30
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 13 of 30
who have physical bodies and some large percentage of them are
fairly interested in keeping those bodies healthy and have
purchased – qualified themselves by purchasing products from
other health companies, and therefore, their names are available to
my clients to rent and to mail my package to.
That’s a huge market, and so I’m willing to take $30.00 a 1,000
royalty when I write a package for the health market. On the other
hand, the investment information market is substantially smaller,
and so I require $50.00 a 1,000 royalty to write a package for an
investment information product, a newsletter or a book –
something on general investments.
If it’s on personal finance – again, everyone has money and there
are huge lists at Rodale and Bodrum of people who qualify
themselves of buying books and newsletters on, you know: how to
save money; how to cut your monthly budget; how to save more;
how to get outta debt; how to cut your taxes; those kinds of things.
Then again, the smaller royalty is in order because of the size of
the universe.
Bob Bly: 	Though – is the money you get paid upfront in advance against
royalty or just a flat fee that’s in addition to the royalty?
Clayton Makepeace: 	I use several different arrangements. Quite often, I use the money
upfront as an – I offer for the money upfront to be an advance
against the royalties, and I receive half when they reserve my time,
up to two years in advance and they only have an opening in 2007.
But if they want that opening, they’re going to have to pay me
$12,500.00 today, which is half of the $25,000.00 advance, and
then the balance, when I either begin work, in some cases – that’s
with clients who know me well [Laughter].
When I deliver a first draft, in other cases, for people who know
me pretty well, but still have some reservations as to whether I’ll
actually deliver [Laughter] copy or not. 	Or on approved final
copy for the clients I haven’t worked for before and need that
assurance that not only will I deliver the copy, but it’ll be powerful
when I do.
Another arrangement is the fee plus royalty, and that’s when
you’re gettin’ cocky. That’s when you’ve been for a while and
you say, “Look, you know, there’s no point in an advance. You
know this is gonna earn out. I’ve gotta fantastic track record.” I

-- 13 of 30 --

The Profit Center 	1_Kick_Your_Business_Into_Hyper-drive 	Page 14 of 30
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 14 of 30
you’re Gary Bencivenga writing a financial package – which,
thank God, he’s not doin’ anymore. [Laughter]
He was the stiff competitor.
Bob Bly: 	[Laughter]
Clayton Makepeace: 	Or Jim Rutz in the financial or health areas or me, in the
supplement areas, and you ask for a fee plus royalty. So the fee is
free and clear of any future deductions, you know, for royalties.
So you get to keep the fee, and then, beginning with the very first
piece mailed, you’d begin to receiving your royalty.
Bob Bly: 	Now, you also mentioned to me that sometimes you get – you have
a commission that’s based or a fee that’s based on a percentage of
not the – it’s not based on the number mailed, but it’s based on the
actual sales of the product. How does that work?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Well, on a lotta the projects that we – you’re going to be doing as a
copywriter – well, they fall into two categories. First category are
direct mail packages, Internet promotions and so forth, that are
designed to generate new customers.
So the copy that you’re writing is going to be sent out to prospects
that are not currently customers of your client, and those mailings
and Internet promotions are quite often done on a breakeven basis.
In other words, the client is willing to continue mailing a copy so
long as he gets his marketing costs back –
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	From the promotion and some clients are willing even to lose a
little bit of money.
Bob Bly: 	Yeah, I can name several.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah. So what does that mean? Well, it means that they’re gonna
be mailing max quantities, right? And it means that they’re going
to be – if they’re shooting for breakeven, it means, essentially, that
100 percent of the revenues that promotion generates are going to
be plowed back into the mail.
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Okay. So you’re talkin’ huge quantities, and on deals like that,
you wanna tie your royalties to the number of pieces mailed. And

-- 14 of 30 --

The Profit Center 	1_Kick_Your_Business_Into_Hyper-drive 	Page 15 of 30
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 15 of 30
in the old days, we always talk in “per 1,000”; today, a lotta people
talk about “percent per piece mail.”
Bob Bly: 	Right, they’ll say – you said $10.00 to $30.00 per 1,000, which is a
$0.01 to $0.03 a piece.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Exactly, and for the smaller markets, like the financial market,
$0.05 a piece. A few years ago, though, Wiese Research – a great
client of mine, and Martin’s a great friend, hired me to do some
promotions to his customer file.
Well, he only had 120,000 customers, and it would take me just as
long to write the promotion for his customers, as it would to write
a promotion to generate new customers, that I knew he was
mailing 18,000,000 pieces a year for – of my packages _________
-
Bob Bly: 	Right, so that promotion goin’ to the customer, if he sends it to
every customer, it’s only mailing 120,000 pieces.
Clayton Makepeace: 	That’s right –
Bob Bly: 	So even if you’re makin’ $0.02 or $0.05 a piece, it’s not significant
to you.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Exactly, and so what I did was I just – I said to him, “Hey, Martin,
look at this. You know, you’re payin’ $500.00 per 1,000 to mail
your direct mail pieces to new customers. My royalty is $50.00 a
1,000” – roughly 10 percent of the money that promotion will
generate because he was mailing at breakeven.
And so I said, “I will be more than happy to write for your house
file for ten percent of the revenue that my promotion generates” –
Bob Bly: 	Gross revenue.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Of the gross revenue that my promotion generates; that’s correct.
Now, in his case gross revenue and net revenue were very similar
because was selling electronically delivered product.
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	He had no printing costs or mailing costs in terms of fulfillment.
So – and no marketing costs because everything we were writing
we were email blast –

-- 15 of 30 --

The Profit Center 	1_Kick_Your_Business_Into_Hyper-drive 	Page 16 of 30
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 16 of 30
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	And so I said, “You’re payin’ me ten percent of revenues in the
mail. I think you should pay me ten percent of revenues when I
promote on the Internet.” And he said, “Yes,” and the rest is
history.
Bob Bly: 	What about – you mentioned to me, in conversation, something
called an “override”; how does that work?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah, this is not always easy to do and some clients will resist it,
but once ‘ya have some winners with the client – I did this with
Wiese and it worked out wonderfully. I went to my client and I
said, “Look, I’m generating 10,000 – 15,000 – sometimes 20,000
new customers for you every month.”
“And I’m doing it for $50.00 a 1,000 royalty. You’re breaking
even on those names; those new customers are costing you
nothing. Then, right away, you’re turning around and you’re
creaming those names.”
“You’re mailing strong electronic promotions to them, offering
$5,000.00 trading services - $10,000.00 trading services and other
very lucrative products.” And I said, “You know, I think I deserve
a portion of the revenue that my customers, that I generated for
you, are bringing you.”
And I had the added benefit that – Dan Rosenthal, and I were kind
of tag teaming with client at the time, and Dan was doing some
new customer acquisition stuff, I was doing some. Dan was doing
some backend stuff and now Martin wanted me to do some. And
so I said, “Look, why don’t we do this.”
“Let me take over the front end entirely, kay? I’ll get you all your
new customers. But then I’m not gonna, you know, stand around
while you and Dan make all the money on the backend. I want an
override of five percent on every purchase the customers, I
generate from you, make.”
“And then I’ll be outta your hair on the backend and so you need
me – if you want me to come in, but I won’t be hounding you to
give me backend assignments ‘cause that’s where the money is.”
And he said, “Okay,” and so for doing absolutely nothing extra,
other than writing the promotion for the front end I was doing
anyway, I collected a nice five percent override on all backend
sales.

-- 16 of 30 --

The Profit Center 	1_Kick_Your_Business_Into_Hyper-drive 	Page 17 of 30
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 17 of 30
Bob Bly: 	So I guess these options – does the copywriter just – especially if
you’re a new copywriter, do you just pick one of these options and
tell the client, “This is what I charge”? Or do you give the client
several choices?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Well, if I were beginning today, I would probably begin with an
advance plus royalty arrangement, and I would probably start out
offering or asking on – asking for something in the range of
$15,000.00 advance – $12,000.00 to $15,000.00 advance against a
royalty of $12.00 to $15.00 a 1,000.
If you want to – and this is important to understand, if your copy
doesn’t kick butt your royalty costs the client nothing, zero. There
are some clients out there that will say, “Well, we don’t pay new
writers that.” Well, that is just specious.
The fact of the matter is, that, as a new writer, you can go in and
you can say, you know, “I wanna $50.00 a 1,000 royalty.” And if
the client says, “Well, we don’t pay new writers that,” you just say,
“Well, you know, you won’t have to pay it if I don’t beat what
your experienced writers are doing”; you know?
“If I do, I’m worth it. If I don’t, it costs you nothing. What do ‘ya
have to lose”; all right?
Bob Bly: 	Interesting point – way to present that.
Clayton Makepeace: 	And then a lot of people will say to you – and this was actually
said to me, by a good friend who’s a marketing director, the other
day, “But, you know, new writers aren’t consistent, and so we may
– we pay the higher royalties to experienced writers and writers
that we have a track record with, because we know we have a
higher percent chance of getting a control when we work with that
person.”
Again, a totally specious argument because consistency has
nothing to do with it, the royalty is not paid unless the package
wins. If your package generates a profit or generates a customer,
at breakeven, your copy is worth exactly the same to that client as
copies that work not that well, or that well written by an A-level
writer.
So negotiating down advances, or flat fees, because of the lack of
consistency or history or track record or experience of a young
writer, that is an arguable point; it’s logical. But when someone

-- 17 of 30 --

The Profit Center 	1_Kick_Your_Business_Into_Hyper-drive 	Page 18 of 30
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 18 of 30
[Laughter] tells you that it’s a customer you generate doesn’t have
the same value to that company as a customer that Gary
Bencivenga or I or Jim Rutz or Arthur Johnson generate, I’m sorry,
that’s – I he’s just hagglin’ at that point.
He’s horsetradin’, and he doesn’t believe it [Laughter] either.
Bob Bly: 	Got it. By the way, we just –
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah –
Bob Bly: 	Had question emailed us. You had used the term – you had
suggested that you would approach a large publisher and tell them,
“Hey, you helped me finish this promotion. You copy chiefed
me.” And the student emailed us and says, “What do ‘ya mean by
‘copy chief’?”
Clayton Makepeace: 	Okay, the – a copy chief is simply somebody, either a mentor, an
A-level writer or, in some cases, a marketing director at a client
company, who will – acknowledging that you are a junior writer or
just beginning – or even an experienced writer but new to this
field, who will agree to work with you to develop the theme, the
headline, the organization as a package, and who will work with
you to make the package successful.
He’ll critique each draft, we call it “critting”; you know, he’ll crit
each draft. Dan Rosenthal is the most famous critter in the
universe; the guy is brutal. He takes a sadistic joy in absolutely
eviscerating copy; in fact, his email address is “copy ogre.” But –
Bob Bly: 	[Laughter]
Clayton Makepeace: 	Anyway, the – a copy chief will take you and take you through a
series of projects. If he’s not a client who’s doing this, if he’s a A-
level writer, or a coach, he’ll typically take a portion of the
advance and a portion of the royalty. Depending on your
experience level, that portion could be substantial or less.
You know, if you’re pretty close, and he knows that he has a good
chance of earning royalties when works with you, then he’ll be
likely to ask for a lower percentage of the royalty in advance. But
on the other hand, he sees 27 drafts ahead of him and even then,
you know, a so-so chance of getting a royalty out of it, then he’ll
want more.

-- 18 of 30 --

The Profit Center 	1_Kick_Your_Business_Into_Hyper-drive 	Page 19 of 30
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 19 of 30
Bob Bly: 	So what’s the best way for someone who is a fairly much of a
beginner and doesn’t have a big track record and hasn’t written a
lotta promotions? Let’s say you wanted to go over the newsletter
field and ‘ya haven’t worked for a newsletter publisher before, is
your best bet – if I could do one thing, is it go find an A-level
writer and apprentice to him?
Or is it to do as you said and write – pick a product from a major
publisher, write a package and show it to them; like, show Ed
Elliott a promotion for one of his supplements.
Clayton Makepeace: 	I really think it’s kind of all of the above. It’s like deep sea
fishing, you’ve gotta have a lotta lines in the water. In “The Total
Package” – actually, I think it was the landing page for this
teleconference.
I talked about the six-step program I use to recruit new clients back
in the very late ‘70s, very early ‘80s, when I first went out and
began freelancing; and that worked like a charm and that it, not
only got me assignments, it got my name out there, and I was able
to get samples out.
So I had that going on. At the same time, I was calling publishers I
wanted to work for, and then insulting their packages and
challenging them to let me beat them, as we discussed earlier with
Johnny Johnson at Research Publications.
And, at the same time, I was taking assignments from clients I
really didn’t want, and that were a pain in the neck, but it gave me
a sample to show. So –
Bob Bly: 	So how important is it at the beginning to get samples? Is that
more important than the money you make for the first six months
or year?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah, it depends on where you are in the process really. But
samples are, at least, as expensive – I mean, as valuable as the
money in the early going. Obviously, you can’t eat samples and if,
like me, you’re trying to support a wife and a couple of kids and a
world’s dumbest cocker spaniel while trying to get your business
going, you obviously need the [Laughter] income as well.
But samples are money in the bank; they’re absolute money in the
bank, and the more that you can put in your portfolio – and, again,
at first, I had samples where I did a promotion for a point-of-use
water heater. The kind of water heater they put in hot tubs.

-- 19 of 30 --

The Profit Center 	1_Kick_Your_Business_Into_Hyper-drive 	Page 20 of 30
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 20 of 30
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	I had an airplane – so I had one for a shop that sold exotic desserts
in Beverly Hills; you know, all kinds of dumb stuff. I had some
fundraising samples that I had done to raise funds for Proposition
13 in California, and so, you know, I showed those to my
newsletter clients and –
Bob Bly: 	Did they react – respond to that –
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah, they –
Bob Bly: 	Or did they say?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Look, you know, anybody who does direct marketing can look at
copy and know whether it’s hot copy or not. They’ll read it and
they’ll know intuitively, “This makes me wanna buy, this makes
me excited about the product. I can see this person writing for my
newsletter and doin’ a bang up job.” So –
Bob Bly: 	I think that’s a major point, and I wanna just emphasize it. So
you’re saying that you can impress, let’s say – let’s just take
newsletters as an example. You can impress a major publisher of
newsletters with really good copy, even if that copy is not selling
newsletters.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Absolutely, absolutely. You know, ‘cause young copywriters send
me their samples all the time, and I can literally spend 30 seconds
scanning a sample and know right where this person is and exactly
how much work it would take on my part, to get a finished package
outta him that will be winner.
Him or her [Laughter]; ‘cause I did this with two ladies very
recently. And I don’t care if it’s about tapes or records or
lawnmowers or, you know, vitamin supplements. It doesn’t
matter.
Either the – if the person has a handle on the dominant emotion of
their market; if they’re speaking to the benefits in a exciting and
compelling way; and if they’re making – not ordering, feel like the
dumbest thing I could do, then this is somebody that I wanna be in
business with.
And so absolutely, I would take every assignment I could get. I
would makeup assignments [Laughter] if I couldn’t get them. But

-- 20 of 30 --

The Profit Center 	1_Kick_Your_Business_Into_Hyper-drive 	Page 21 of 30
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 21 of 30
it’s a stepping stone process at first, to some degree. Every once in
a while, you’re gonna hit – like, with the six-step program that I’ve
outlined in “The Total Package” landing page.
Every once in a while, somebody will say, “You know what? I do
think you can do this, and here’s what we pay. Is that okay with
you? And let’s do one together and see what happens.” And the
more lines you have in the water, the more likely that is to happen
for you.
Sooner or later we’ll cover everything in “The Total Package,” but
I think the people who were kind enough to pay to attend this call,
should be the ones that get the benefit of those answers.
Bob Bly: 	Well, let’s get to one of those questions that just came in ‘cause it
was about what we were discussing. Are royalties based on the
number of pieces mailed or on the response to the mailing?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Okay, the response to the mailing determines the number of pieces
mailed.
Bob Bly: 	Right, because if it doesn’t do well, they’re not gonna keep mailing
it.
Clayton Makepeace: 	That’s right. Clients – as I mentioned earlier, a client has,
basically, a redline. His boss or his accounting department has told
him that your – and by “him” I mean him or her as the marketing
manager, has given that marketing manager an objective; and the
objective might be, for example, “I want new customers for free.”
Which means, “When I do my promotion, I want all the money I
spend on that promotion back, and I wanna new customer”; all
right?
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	And so that means that that marketing manager can mail just – you
know, mail every mailing list out there that will come in at
breakeven or, you know, factors mailing so you mail some losers,
too.
But the overall affect of the mailing is return on investment is 100
percent; he gets 100 percent of his money back. And, then others
will get more aggressive – right? And say, “We’ll lose a little bit
of money because we have this great backend program we’re
gonna be selling.”

-- 21 of 30 --

The Profit Center 	1_Kick_Your_Business_Into_Hyper-drive 	Page 22 of 30
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 22 of 30
“So we don’t mind coming in at 80 percent or 90 percent because
we know in the first 60 days we’ll recover that money,” and then
that clients with us 7 years and he’s gonna spend money like crazy.
So they have a different criteria.
Still, others, and these are the ones I don’t understand, they say,
“We wanna make money on every new client,” and those are the
ones you’ve gotta be careful about because if a guy tells you that
he wants to make a profit on every client, he wants a return on
investment for the mailing of 120 percent or 140 percent, then that
means you can mail a lot fewer pieces.
So the relationship with – between response and mail and
promotion side it’s a direct relationship, and the larger – the higher
the response or the – let’s say, for example, a guy’s mission in life
is to breakeven on new customers, and he mails your package and
it pulls at 300 or 400 percent return on investment on test lists.
Most clients will test the better outside files on a new package to
lower their risk of losing money when they test new packaging,
and let’s say you pull 400 percent on his best outside files. Well
then he goes back to the – his office and sits down with his mailing
list history, and he looks at how every other list he’s ever mailed,
indexes against the list that he just tested your package to.
And he immediately knows how many 1,000,000 pieces of that
package he can mail; right?
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	So the – your response percentage determines mail size, and so
you’re being rewarded for the affect –
Bob Bly: 	Right –
Clayton Makepeace: 	________ -
Bob Bly: 	But the royalty, itself, is actually based on the mail size, the
number of pieces mailed –
[Crosstalk]
Bob Bly: 	As you would put it, it’s $10.00 – $20.00 – $30.00 – $50.00 per
1,000 pieces mailed.

-- 22 of 30 --

The Profit Center 	1_Kick_Your_Business_Into_Hyper-drive 	Page 23 of 30
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 23 of 30
Clayton Makepeace: 	That’s right –
[Crosstalk]
Bob Bly: 	Or $0.01 to $0.05 per piece –
Clayton Makepeace: 	It’s sink or severs to chance. You’ve got – the response rate you
produce, determines the size of the mailing, and your royalty is
connected to the size of the mailing, either per piece mailed or on a
per 1,000 basis; and so that’s how it works.
Bob Bly: 	Now, in our original promotion for this conference, we had a bullet
point that I don’t think we’ve answered yet. The bullet – and you
wrote it, actually, was how being a direct response bully can make
you rich. So –
Clayton Makepeace: 	[Laughter] –
Bob Bly: 	Who’s the bully? I –
Clayton Makepeace: 	[Laughter] –
Bob Bly: 	Suspect it’s you –
Clayton Makepeace: 	[Laughter] Yeah, well, actually, that was a kind of flowery way of
discussing something we have talked about, and that’s looking –
one of the best things you can do as a copywriter – and it’s not
easy for people who are starting out because money’s tight.
But get on the mailing lists of the competitors of the companies
that you wanna work for; if you wanna work, for example, in the
financial publishing industry, cough up the bucks and take a one
year subscription to one of the newsletters offered by each of the
major mailers.
Bob Bly: 	And it doesn’t cost a lot ‘cause each of them has a front end
newsletter that’s only $49.00 a year or so.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Right, some of them are as high as $99.00 –
Bob Bly: 	Yeah, but that’s it.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah, that’s it, and you get on those lists; and then what happens is
every time that list is rented to a competitor – every time Agora –
say you subscribe to ____ -

-- 23 of 30 --

The Profit Center 	1_Kick_Your_Business_Into_Hyper-drive 	Page 24 of 30
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 24 of 30
Bob Bly: 	“Oxford Club.”
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah, or “Oxford Club” or “Strategic Investing” over at Agora.
Well, you know, next month – within 30 days, you’re gonna get a
promotion from Phillips because Agora rents their lists to Phillips,
and you’re gonna start seein’ all of the promotions pieces that
Phillips does for their product.
You’re gonna see the promotion pieces that everybody Agora rents
that list to due for their product –
Bob Bly: 	Right –
Clayton Makepeace: 	Your mailbox is gonna start filling up, you know. You’re gonna
be disappointed on days you go to the mailbox and there’s nothing
there. I mean, it almost never happens; you’ll be getting a lot of
mail.
And so that gives you an opportunity to see everyone’s promotion
pieces out there, and not just their controls, you’ll be seeing test
panels as well.
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Keep that in mind when you’re looking at these things ‘cause you
may look at some of these and say, “Man, this thing really stinks. I
could beat the livin’ daylights out of it.” When you call ‘em up
and tell ‘em that, they may say, “Yeah, I know. That was a tested
bomb.” You [Laughter] know? But – so be prepared for that.
Bob Bly: 	Yep.
Clayton Makepeace: 	But look through those packages and then be a bully. Pick the
wimpiest kid on the block, the one that you’re sure you can whip,
in a New York minute, and approach the client with that and say,
“What were you thinking?” [Laughter] You know? And explain
to him, why you can beat it.
And I tell ‘ya it’s amazing. One of my major clients sent me two
promotion pieces that they had mailed over – within the last year
and it hadn’t worked and asked me to critique them and tell them
why I thought they didn’t work.
And I spent maybe a half hour just going through these pieces and
looking at them, and they were copy chiefed by strong, creative
people internally; and they missed the mark at almost every level.

-- 24 of 30 --

The Profit Center 	1_Kick_Your_Business_Into_Hyper-drive 	Page 25 of 30
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 25 of 30
And I’m not gonna say which client that was, but it knocked my
socks off because I have very, very high respect for the people who
copy chiefed and supervised the production of this package.
So just because a company is huge and just because they’re
mailing something doesn’t mean it’s great and doesn’t mean it’s
working well. So, you know, go ahead and be a bully; pick on it –
the weakest packages out there.
And then go – and come up with a plan on how you would beat it,
and then go to the group publisher or the marketing director at that
company and strike up a conversation about it. That’s worked very
well for me many times in the past.
Bob Bly: 	What great advice. Another question that just came out this says,
how do we go about finding the client companies that use and need
copywriters? And I think you have kind of answered that just now,
didn’t ‘ya?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Well, there is several ways actually –
Bob Bly: 	Right –
Clayton Makepeace: 	But I tell ‘ya what, you know, in I found, you know, first of all,
that niche writers make the most money. It’s like a cardiologist
makes more money –
Bob Bly: 	Specialist.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yes. Right, a cardiologist makes more money than a general
practitioner –
Bob Bly: 	Right –
Clayton Makepeace: 	Right? And in copywriting it’s really the same thing. If you have
a specialty in financial writing, a lot goes with that; I mean, you –
not – you don’t have to just be a good copywriter, you need to
know the difference between a mutual fund and a exchange traded
fund; you need to know what price earnings ratios and price book
ratios are; you need to know about why the market goes up, why it
goes down; you need to know about economic trends.
There’s a lotta specialized knowledge that goes into that, and the
same is true in the health area. You don’t have to necessarily
know about which alternative remedies work the best because the

-- 25 of 30 --

The Profit Center 	1_Kick_Your_Business_Into_Hyper-drive 	Page 26 of 30
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 26 of 30
editor will tell ‘ya that. But when he starts talking about an
embolism, you need to know what that is.
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	And when he starts talking about a cardiac infarction, you need to
know he means heart attack, you know, so there’s a lot goes into
becoming a specialist in each of these fields.
Bob Bly: 	But to answer this question, specifically, of where do ‘ya find the
clients, let me give several suggestions; see if you agree. First of
all, there are directories that cover the direct marketing industry,
and the – there’s one called the, Directory of Major Mailers,
which, you know, is a massive book that lists almost every
company that does direct marketing in any volume.
I believe it’s available if you search “Directory of Major Mailers”
on the Internet, you can get it online, and you can purchase it on
CD or in paper; and that’ll give ‘ya thousands of companies, and
you can find out exactly what they sell and what they mail and
how many packages.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Absolutely. I’m looking at it now; it’s a target marketing group
and it’s – you can go to w-w-w “major mailers” dot com. Book’s
about 3 inches thick, and not only does it tell you, you know, who
the major mailers are, but it tells you what they’re [Laughter]
mailing.
Bob Bly: 	And notice folks, Clayton doesn’t need clients; just the opposite.
And he owns a Directory of Major Mailers.
Clayton Makepeace: 	[Laughter] Well, actually, I wanna offer this to “Total Package”
subscribers, which is why I bought it.
Bob Bly: 	Okay.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah.
Bob Bly: 	Well, that’s cool, and then, if you’re interested, for example, in
newsletter publishers, you can search w-w-w dot “newsletters” dot
org; and that’s the website of NEPA which is the major trade
association for business and consumer newsletter publishers. So
those are two sources you can use.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Right, also the Direct Marketing Association –

-- 26 of 30 --

The Profit Center 	1_Kick_Your_Business_Into_Hyper-drive 	Page 27 of 30
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 27 of 30
Bob Bly: 	The DMA.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Right, ‘ya have – I’m not that familiar with their stuff; I haven’t
looked at it in a long time ‘cause I haven’t been beating the bushes
for a long time. But they do conferences and they quite often have
directories that they exhibitors, and I’m sure they also have a
directory of members.
Bob Bly: 	Right, and ‘ya have a local chapter of the Direct Marketing
Association, you join that local chapter – it’s a great way to
network, and usually when you join a local chapter, they give you
the membership directory, which will typically have 100 or 200
potential clients, with all their information listed.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Right. But getting back to what I was saying, as you go through
this, it would be one thing _____ this Directory of Major Mailers,
for example, goes to – lists just about everybody out there. I
would just suggest that you pick a niche, educate yourself in it and
focus on those niches.
Bob Bly: 	Definitely. Look it out what’s mailed to you and what turns you
on; like, Clayton is very into financial and alternative supplements
and some people are interested in commodities trading and options
trading. The more you – I’ve always found this to be true, too.
Specialists have an easier time getting work and are paid more than
generalists.
Clayton Makepeace: 	____ Arthur Johnson got started, really, at Franklin Mint selling
collectibles, and that was his first experience, and he really made a
name for himself in that field.
Bob Bly: 	That is a big niche, collectibles.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Eric Buetell who’s one of the all-time great writers. He’s in the
pantheon; he’s in a hall of fame; four or five best writers of all
time. He writes soft offer, his specialty – he writes everything, but
his specialty, what he almost never misses at, is a soft offer one-
shot book promotion; and he’s produced many of the biggest
winners that Rodale and Bodrum have ever put out. So.
Bob Bly: 	So you see, it can really specialize even deeper; not just books, but
soft offer one-shot book promotions.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Right.

-- 27 of 30 --

The Profit Center 	1_Kick_Your_Business_Into_Hyper-drive 	Page 28 of 30
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 28 of 30
Bob Bly: 	We’re just about outta time, we have two minutes. I wanna ask
one last question, and then we can head toward wrapping up. In
the promotion we sent out, you also mentioned the No. 1 blunder
new copywriters’ make that turns clients off like crazy every time
–
Clayton Makepeace: 	[Laughter] –
Bob Bly: 	What is that?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Being too available. I saw a promotion – it was an ad that ran in, I
think it was ZN News –
Bob Bly: 	We talked about this, I – we should not mention the person’s name.
Clayton Makepeace: 	No, we won’t, and the ad, basically, was – you know, this a
copywriter promoting himself; and when you promote yourself,
like that, broadly, what you’re – you’re sending a message that,
basically, you’re available. And, you know, clients are kinda like
– [Laughter] Rodney Dangerfield once said that, “I wouldn’t” –
“Why would I join any clubs that would accept me as a member?”
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	[Laughter] You know? Well, clients have the same question in
their minds; they’re saying, “Why would I hire any copywriter
who has immediate time in his schedule?” And so ‘ya have to
have a reason why you’re soliciting business now.
And one of the things that I always did when I was trying to sell
my schedule, and I, you know, I wanted, you know, to get some
advances in to pay the bills and to get some bookings, is – I would
contact clients and say, “Look, I fill my schedule just twice a
year,” you know?
“And I’m booked for the next six months” – or three months or
whatever the time period is, “But I’m booking my schedule up for
the first six months of next year. I’m sending this letter to seven”
– or nine or twelve or whatever it was, “Other clients that I work
with and it’s – the slots are going, you know, first come, first
serve.” So ‘ya – in other words, ‘ya have to have a rationale.
Bob Bly: 	There has to be a reason.

-- 28 of 30 --

The Profit Center 	1_Kick_Your_Business_Into_Hyper-drive 	Page 29 of 30
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 29 of 30
Clayton Makepeace: 	There has to be a reason why – you don’t wanna be the, you know,
the ugliest girl [Laughter] at the dance. You wanna be the one that
everybody has to hustle to get on your dance card –
Bob Bly: 	The late Howard Jensen called this “the busy doctor syndrome.”
He said, you know, “Clients always wanna deal with those who
feel busy and successful, not that those who seem to be desperate
and need the work.”
Clayton Makepeace: 	Exactly. _____ -
Bob Bly: 	We’re outta time, but it’s – my one distress is that I think we
coulda gone on another two or three hours. Will we do more of
these with you? What’s gonna be our opportunity? I feel like we
didn’t finish, like we’re just scratching the surface.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah, I think so, too, and I’ve got like 21 more things I wanted to
cover [Laughter] on this call. Plus, we have all of the questions.
Bob, if you’d be willing to do it, I’d like to start doing this on a
regular basis.
Bob Bly: 	Okay, why don’t we talk about that, and then we – whatever we
decide, we can send out an email to the people here and to the
people whose – get “The Total Package.” And let’s do that, let’s
do a series of calls so we can get into a lot of this in much more
detail.
Clayton Makepeace: 	I would like to –
Bob Bly: 	I’m thinkin’ –
Clayton Makepeace: 	Do that –
Bob Bly: 	Three or –
Clayton Makepeace: 	Too –
Bob Bly: 	Four calls would be good.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Oh, absolutely. And one other thing I’d like to ask the group.
Guys, I know what I’m doin’ when it comes to writing copy, but I
– when it comes to being a coach in print, I’m an absolute
neophyte. And [Laughter] so I would deeply appreciate it if you
would take the trouble, in the next day or two, to click on the
“feedback” button at the bottom of “The Total Package” E-zine.

Where it says, “Comments or Article Idea,” that will bring up an
email form addressed to “feedback” at “Makepeace Total Package”
dot com, and let us know how we did on this call. If there were
things that we didn’t cover you would of liked us to, let us know
that because we’ll certainly consider those things for future calls.
Also, just kinda give us a feeling of how we’re doing and any
suggestions that you would have for us as to how we might
improve. You’d be doing me a great service; I’d appreciate it.
Bob Bly: 	Great. Well, Clayton, I thought it was very valuable; I learned a
lot. In other words, from my vantage point, I’m just a six-figure
copywriter, so any time I can learn from a seven-figure, I’m happy
as a clam.
Clayton Makepeace: 	[Laughter] –
Bob Bly: 	Julie, I wanna thank you for making all this happen.
Julia: 	Thanks, no problem. Actually, I did wanna say, as I am
monitoring the questions that have come through, people seem
really pleased.
Bob Bly: 	Good, so let’s talk about, offline, what we can do to continue and
answer everyone’s questions ‘cause I think there’s a lot of
unanswered questions. And Clay, you have a list of 21 things that
you wanna talk about, and I have a list of 10 – 15 things I wanna
ask you.
Clayton Makepeace: 	[Laughter] Great.
Bob Bly: 	All right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	All right, thank you –
Bob Bly: 	Thanks guys.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Thank you everybody for being online –
Bob Bly: 	Yeah. Bye-bye.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Buh-bye.
